note: Used for a farm or agricultural holding where crops and animals are raised.
exampleFrench: La ferme familiale produit du lait et des légumes toute l'année.
exampleEnglish: The family farm produces milk and vegetables all year.
imagePrompt: A warm editorial illustration of a family farm with a barn, fields, and morning light.

note: These related words describe someone who works the land or runs a farm.
exampleFrench: L'agriculteur commence sa journée avant le lever du soleil.
exampleEnglish: The farmer starts the day before sunrise.
imagePrompt: A farmer in boots walking between rows of crops at sunrise, painted in a soft modern style.

note: A collective expression for animals kept on a farm such as cows, rabbits, ducks, and chickens.
exampleFrench: Les enfants adorent donner à manger aux animaux de la ferme.
exampleEnglish: The children love feeding the farm animals.
imagePrompt: A lively farmyard with cows, rabbits, ducks, dogs, cats, and chickens in a bright illustration.

note: A common grain crop used for bread and flour.
exampleFrench: Le blé devient doré juste avant la récolte.
exampleEnglish: The wheat turns golden just before harvest.
imagePrompt: Golden wheat fields moving in the wind under a pale summer sky.

note: Used for maize or corn plants and kernels.
exampleFrench: Le maïs pousse très vite pendant les semaines chaudes.
exampleEnglish: Corn grows very quickly during the warm weeks.
imagePrompt: Tall green corn plants in neat rows with warm late-afternoon sunlight.

note: Both can refer to vacation time, while congés often sounds more administrative or work-related.
exampleFrench: Je prends des congés en août pour partir en vacances.
exampleEnglish: I'm taking time off in August to go on holiday.
imagePrompt: A suitcase by a train window with sunglasses and tickets, bright summer travel mood.

note: An imperative telling one person to move faster or stop wasting time.
exampleFrench: Dépêche-toi, nous allons manquer le bus !
exampleEnglish: Hurry up, we are going to miss the bus!
imagePrompt: A student running toward a bus stop while checking a watch, energetic comic-style scene.

note: Used when the skin becomes red or painful after too much sun exposure.
exampleFrench: Après la plage, il a eu un coup de soleil sur les épaules.
exampleEnglish: After the beach, he got a sunburn on his shoulders.
imagePrompt: A beach scene with someone applying aloe vera to sunburned shoulders, clean editorial illustration.

note: Usually mentioned when talking about sun protection or skin damage.
exampleFrench: Cette crème protège bien contre les ultraviolets.
exampleEnglish: This cream protects well against ultraviolet rays.
imagePrompt: A sunscreen bottle, sunglasses, and a bright sun icon in a summer safety poster style.

note: A common adverb meaning at the present time.
exampleFrench: Actuellement, je révise mon vocabulaire de français chaque soir.
exampleEnglish: Currently, I review my French vocabulary every evening.
imagePrompt: A desk with open notebooks, tea, and evening study light, calm academic mood.

note: Used for grapevines or the vineyard area where they are grown.
exampleFrench: Le vignoble de Bordeaux attire des visiteurs du monde entier.
exampleEnglish: The Bordeaux vineyard attracts visitors from around the world.
imagePrompt: Rows of grapevines on rolling hills near Bordeaux, elegant travel-poster composition.

note: Used for grapes as fruit or harvest produce.
exampleFrench: Le raisin est très sucré cette année.
exampleEnglish: The grapes are very sweet this year.
imagePrompt: A close-up bunch of ripe purple grapes in soft vineyard light.

note: The standard word for wine.
exampleFrench: Ce restaurant sert un très bon vin rouge.
exampleEnglish: This restaurant serves a very good red wine.
imagePrompt: A refined table scene with a glass of red wine and candlelight, tasteful illustration.

note: Used for a music company that signs and promotes artists.
exampleFrench: La chanteuse a signé avec un grand label musical.
exampleEnglish: The singer signed with a major record label.
imagePrompt: A young singer in a studio meeting music executives, sleek contemporary illustration.

note: A physical record played on a turntable.
exampleFrench: Il collectionne les disques vinyles des années quatre-vingt.
exampleEnglish: He collects vinyl records from the eighties.
imagePrompt: A turntable spinning a colorful vinyl record in a retro living room.

note: The same abbreviation is used in French.
exampleFrench: J'ai encore quelques CD dans ma voiture.
exampleEnglish: I still have a few CDs in my car.
imagePrompt: A stack of compact discs catching rainbow reflections in sunlight.

english: to be noticed by a professional
note: Used when talent or work gets attention from an expert in the field.
exampleFrench: Elle espère être remarquée par un professionnel pendant le concours.
exampleEnglish: She hopes to be noticed by a professional during the competition.
imagePrompt: A performer on stage while a judge takes notes in the front row.

note: Remarques can be neutral comments, while reproches often means criticism or blame.
exampleFrench: Le chef a fait des remarques sur notre présentation.
exampleEnglish: The boss made remarks about our presentation.
imagePrompt: An office meeting where a manager gives feedback to a team around a table.

note: Used for beginning again after a change, setback, or major transition.
exampleFrench: Après la crise, le pays veut prendre un nouveau départ.
exampleEnglish: After the crisis, the country wants to make a fresh start.
imagePrompt: A sunrise over a city skyline with people walking forward, hopeful civic-poster mood.

note: Ouvrage often sounds a bit more formal than livre.
exampleFrench: Cet ouvrage historique est facile à lire malgré sa longueur.
exampleEnglish: This historical work is easy to read despite its length.
imagePrompt: A beautifully bound book open on a wooden desk beside handwritten notes.

note: Copain and copine are casual, while ami and amie are more standard.
exampleFrench: Je retrouve une amie au café après les cours.
exampleEnglish: I'm meeting a friend at the cafe after class.
imagePrompt: Two friends chatting over coffee at a Parisian terrace, friendly and relaxed.

note: Used for a romantic partner, not just an ordinary friend.
exampleFrench: Elle a présenté son petit copain à sa famille.
exampleEnglish: She introduced her boyfriend to her family.
imagePrompt: A young couple meeting family at dinner, warm domestic illustration.

note: A formal connector used to introduce contrast.
exampleFrench: Le film est long. Toutefois, il reste passionnant.
exampleEnglish: The film is long. However, it remains fascinating.
imagePrompt: A stylish movie poster composition with contrasting light and shadow.

note: Useful for adding a side comment or extra supporting point.
exampleFrench: Il parle très bien français. D'ailleurs, il a vécu à Lyon.
exampleEnglish: He speaks French very well. Besides, he lived in Lyon.
imagePrompt: A conversational cafe scene with subtle speech bubbles and map elements of Lyon.

note: A connector used to show result or consequence.
exampleFrench: Le train a été annulé. En conséquence, nous sommes rentrés tard.
exampleEnglish: The train was cancelled. Consequently, we got home late.
imagePrompt: A delayed train board with travelers adjusting plans, clean editorial travel style.

note: Used when a prize, title, or honor is formally given.
exampleFrench: Le jury a décerné le prix du meilleur film à la réalisatrice.
exampleEnglish: The jury awarded the best film prize to the director.
imagePrompt: A film awards stage with spotlight, envelope, and applause.

note: Used when an authority officially orders or declares something.
exampleFrench: La mairie a décrété un couvre-feu après les violences.
exampleEnglish: The city council decreed a curfew after the violence.
imagePrompt: A city at night under curfew with streetlights and official notices, serious news illustration.

note: Used when someone is officially chosen for a role or responsibility.
exampleFrench: Le parti a désigné un nouveau premier ministre.
exampleEnglish: The party appointed a new prime minister.
imagePrompt: A formal political announcement with microphones and a new leader stepping forward.

note: Can refer to broadcasting a TV program or spreading a smell or message.
exampleFrench: Cette chaîne diffuse le match en direct ce soir.
exampleEnglish: This channel is broadcasting the match live tonight.
imagePrompt: A TV control room with live sports footage on multiple screens.

note: Used for a drop of water, liquid, or even a tiny amount.
exampleFrench: Il n'y a plus une goutte d'eau dans son verre.
exampleEnglish: There is not a single drop of water left in his glass.
imagePrompt: A nearly empty glass with one shining drop left at the bottom, cinematic close-up.

note: An idiom for escaping a problem or unpleasant situation by luck.
exampleFrench: Avec cette grève, nous avons eu de la chance et nous sommes passés entre les gouttes.
exampleEnglish: With that strike, we were lucky and narrowly avoided trouble.
imagePrompt: Someone slipping between heavy raindrops with an umbrella, playful metaphorical illustration.

note: Also used figuratively for a tiny splash in a drink in informal speech.
exampleFrench: Une larme a coulé sur sa joue pendant le film.
exampleEnglish: A tear rolled down her cheek during the film.
imagePrompt: A close-up portrait with one tear and soft cinema light reflected in the eye.

note: An idiom meaning it is raining very hard.
exampleFrench: Prends ton parapluie, il pleut des cordes dehors.
exampleEnglish: Take your umbrella, it's raining heavily outside.
imagePrompt: A city street in strong rain with umbrellas bending in the wind, dramatic poster style.

note: Used in tech for systems like Windows or iOS, and metaphorically for systems of exploitation in society.
exampleFrench: Mon PC fonctionne avec le système d'exploitation Windows.
exampleEnglish: My PC runs on the Windows operating system.
imagePrompt: A laptop desktop interface with layered windows and clean modern tech visuals.

note: Used for the state of being alone, often with an emotional weight.
exampleFrench: De plus en plus de personnes souffrent de solitude.
exampleEnglish: More and more people suffer from loneliness.
imagePrompt: A solitary figure in a city apartment at dusk, reflective and gentle illustration.

note: Used when a task or situation feels too difficult to handle.
exampleFrench: Cet exercice est trop difficile, je ne m'en sors pas.
exampleEnglish: This exercise is too difficult, I'm not managing.
imagePrompt: A stressed student at a desk full of notes, trying to solve a hard exercise.

note: Used for bereavement or deep personal loss.
exampleFrench: Après avoir perdu un proche, il a eu besoin de temps pour se reconstruire.
exampleEnglish: After losing a loved one, he needed time to rebuild himself.
imagePrompt: A quiet memorial moment with a framed photo, candlelight, and soft muted tones.

note: A stronger, more personal version of simply being alone.
exampleFrench: Elle est restée toute seule dans l'appartement pendant le week-end.
exampleEnglish: She stayed all alone in the apartment during the weekend.
imagePrompt: A quiet apartment interior at night with one person by the window.

note: A dramatic expression used when a decline has clearly started.
exampleFrench: Pour cette entreprise, cette décision a marqué le début de la fin.
exampleEnglish: For that company, that decision marked the beginning of the end.
imagePrompt: A collapsing corporate skyline with one tower dimming first, symbolic editorial art.

note: Used for cheating in class, a game, or an exam.
exampleFrench: Il a triché pendant le contrôle en regardant le téléphone de son voisin.
exampleEnglish: He cheated during the test by looking at his neighbor's phone.
imagePrompt: A classroom test scene with one student secretly glancing sideways, tense academic mood.

english: to deceive / to cheat on someone
note: Depending on context, tromper means mislead, deceive, or be unfaithful.
exampleFrench: Tromper son conjoint est très grave pour beaucoup de couples.
exampleEnglish: Cheating on your partner is very serious for many couples.
imagePrompt: A dramatic relationship scene with two people in tense conversation at home.

note: Used for someone who lies or the act of lying.
exampleFrench: Je ne lui fais plus confiance, c'est un menteur.
exampleEnglish: I don't trust him anymore, he is a liar.
imagePrompt: A stylized portrait with crossed fingers hidden behind the back, symbolic of lying.

note: The dog breed, not a shepherd from Germany.
exampleFrench: Le berger allemand du voisin aboie quand quelqu'un arrive.
exampleEnglish: The neighbor's German shepherd barks when someone arrives.
imagePrompt: A loyal German shepherd by a garden gate, realistic but warm illustration.

note: Can mean to take an oath or to promise strongly.
exampleFrench: Je te jure que je dis la vérité.
exampleEnglish: I swear to you that I am telling the truth.
imagePrompt: A close-up of someone with a hand on a book, making a solemn promise.

note: Stage can mean internship or practical training depending on context.
exampleFrench: Elle fait un stage en entreprise pendant l'été.
exampleEnglish: She is doing an internship in a company during the summer.
imagePrompt: A young intern in an office with a badge and laptop, modern professional setting.

note: Often confused with stage, which means internship or training period.
exampleFrench: Le concert aura lieu dans un grand stade ce week-end.
exampleEnglish: The concert will take place in a large stadium this weekend.
imagePrompt: A packed stadium lit for a night concert, high-energy wide-angle scene.

note: An idiom for wasting money carelessly.
exampleFrench: Acheter ce gadget inutile, c'est jeter l'argent par les fenêtres.
exampleEnglish: Buying that useless gadget is throwing money away.
imagePrompt: Banknotes flying from an open window in a playful satirical illustration.

note: Râler is very colloquial and often suggests grumbling.
exampleFrench: Il râle toujours quand le métro est en retard.
exampleEnglish: He always complains when the metro is late.
imagePrompt: A commuter grumbling on a delayed metro platform, expressive urban illustration.

note: Used to talk about having a noticeable belly or gaining weight around the stomach.
exampleFrench: Depuis qu'il travaille assis toute la journée, il a pris du ventre.
exampleEnglish: Since he works sitting all day, he has gained a belly.
imagePrompt: A casual lifestyle illustration showing body changes over time, friendly and non-judgmental.

terms: se faire mordre par un chien
english: to bite / to get bitten by a dog
note: Mordre means to bite; se faire mordre means to get bitten.
exampleFrench: Il s'est fait mordre par un chien dans le parc.
exampleEnglish: He got bitten by a dog in the park.
imagePrompt: A park scene with a startled person and a barking dog, clear instructional illustration.

note: Used for jealousy as a feeling or to describe a jealous person.
exampleFrench: Son comportement montre une jalousie maladive.
exampleEnglish: His behavior shows unhealthy jealousy.
imagePrompt: A tense couple scene with a phone on the table and suspicious body language.

note: Used when searching a place, bag, pocket, or someone else's things.
exampleFrench: Il a fouillé dans son sac pour retrouver ses clés.
exampleEnglish: He rummaged through his bag to find his keys.
imagePrompt: Someone searching through a backpack on a hallway bench, candid daily-life illustration.

note: These expressions vary in intensity, with furieux usually sounding strongest.
exampleFrench: Elle est en colère parce qu'on a perdu ses documents.
exampleEnglish: She is angry because her documents were lost.
imagePrompt: A frustrated office worker with scattered papers and strong body language.

note: Figuratively, prendre une gifle can also mean receiving a harsh shock or disappointment.
exampleFrench: Je pensais gagner, mais le résultat a été une vraie gifle.
exampleEnglish: I thought I would win, but the result was a real slap in the face.
imagePrompt: A competition scoreboard revealing an unexpected loss, emotional editorial art.

note: Attachant means endearing or likable despite flaws.
exampleFrench: Ce personnage est bête mais attachant, alors on lui pardonne tout.
exampleEnglish: That character is stupid but lovable, so we forgive him everything.
imagePrompt: A clumsy but charming movie character smiling after making a mistake.

english: to make someone laugh / to laugh
note: Useful for talking about humor and reactions.
exampleFrench: Tu me fais rire avec tes histoires absurdes.
exampleEnglish: You make me laugh with your absurd stories.
imagePrompt: Friends laughing together at a dinner table, spontaneous lively illustration.

note: The emotional pain of missing your home country or home place.
exampleFrench: Quand je suis parti étudier loin, j'ai eu le mal du pays.
exampleEnglish: When I left to study far away, I felt homesick.
imagePrompt: A student in a foreign city looking at family photos, tender homesick atmosphere.

note: A set phrase associated with jewelry, luxury, and permanence.
exampleFrench: Dans la publicité, on répète que les diamants sont éternels.
exampleEnglish: In the advertisement, they keep repeating that diamonds are forever.
imagePrompt: A luxury jewelry ad scene with a diamond ring under dramatic spotlights.

note: Fiançailles means engagement, while bague de fiançailles and alliance refer to rings.
exampleFrench: Il lui a offert une bague de fiançailles au bord de la mer.
exampleEnglish: He gave her an engagement ring by the sea.
imagePrompt: A seaside proposal scene with a ring box and golden evening light.

note: Cacher is transitive, while se cacher means to hide oneself.
exampleFrench: L'enfant aime se cacher derrière le rideau.
exampleEnglish: The child likes to hide behind the curtain.
imagePrompt: A playful indoor hide-and-seek scene with a child behind a curtain.

note: Used when someone leaves abruptly and impolitely.
exampleFrench: Elle est partie sans dire au revoir à personne.
exampleEnglish: She left without saying goodbye to anyone.
imagePrompt: A person slipping out of a gathering while others are still talking, cinematic farewell scene.

note: Connectors used to express cause or reason in French.
exampleFrench: Je suis arrivé en retard au travail, en effet je me suis réveillé en retard ce matin.
exampleEnglish: I arrived late to work, in fact I woke up late this morning.
imagePrompt: A person waking up late and rushing to work.

note: Connectors used to express comparison in French.
exampleFrench: Mon professeur d’université a été gentil avec moi, de la même manière, je suis gentil avec mes étudiants.
exampleEnglish: My university professor was kind to me, in the same way, I am kind to my students.
imagePrompt: Two people being kind to each other.

note: Connectors used to express concession or contrast in French.
exampleFrench: Malgré les efforts diplomatiques, la guerre n'a pas pu être évitée.
exampleEnglish: Despite diplomatic efforts, the war could not be avoided.
imagePrompt: Diplomatic efforts failing to prevent war.

note: Connectors used to express supposition or hypothesis in French.
exampleFrench: En admettant qu’il a échoué, il a démissionné de son emploi.
exampleEnglish: Admitting that he failed, he resigned from his job.
imagePrompt: A person resigning after failure.

note: Connectors used to express consequence or result in French.
exampleFrench: J’ai beaucoup étudié. Par suite, j’ai réussi l’examen.
exampleEnglish: I studied a lot. Consequently, I passed the exam.
imagePrompt: A student studying and then passing.

note: Connectors used to provide explanation or clarification in French.
exampleFrench: Il manque une chose, à savoir ta signature.
exampleEnglish: One thing is missing, namely your signature.
imagePrompt: A document missing a signature.

note: Connectors used to express opposition or contrast in French.
exampleFrench: L’équipe a bien joué, cependant ils ont perdu le match.
exampleEnglish: The team played well, however they lost the match.
imagePrompt: A team playing well but losing.

note: Connectors used to provide examples or illustration in French.
exampleFrench: J’ai visité, entre autres, D.C. et l’Ohio.
exampleEnglish: I visited, among others, D.C. and Ohio.
imagePrompt: Visiting various places.

note: Connectors used to add information in French.
exampleFrench: De plus, nous avons décidé de rester la nuit.
exampleEnglish: Moreover, we decided to stay the night.
imagePrompt: People deciding to stay longer.

note: Connectors used to express purpose or intention in French.
exampleFrench: Il a marché lentement afin que nous puissions marcher ensemble.
exampleEnglish: He walked slowly so that we could walk together.
imagePrompt: People walking together slowly.

note: Connectors used to express restriction or exception in French.
exampleFrench: Tout le monde est venu, hormis Guillaume.
exampleEnglish: Everyone came, except Guillaume.
imagePrompt: Everyone except one person.

note: Connectors used to structure text or speech in French.
exampleFrench: D’abord, je me réveille. Ensuite, je me brosse les dents. Enfin, je vais aller dans mon lit encore.
exampleEnglish: First, I wake up. Then, I brush my teeth. Finally, I go back to bed.
imagePrompt: A daily routine sequence.

note: Relative pronouns used in French after prepositions, with specific forms for de and à.
exampleFrench: Les raisons pour lesquelles nous sommes parties sont confidentielles.
exampleEnglish: The reasons for which we left are confidential.
imagePrompt: Examples of relative pronouns in sentences.
